name: 2A — The Mum at the Gate
headline: The mum who knows something you don't.
body:
The mums whose kids are quietly thriving aren't smarter parents. They've just made one phone call you haven't.

We match every family with a real teacher for weekly 1-on-1 lessons — and a real human education manager who picks up when you ring. No call centre. No chatbot. Just Anna.
audience: Pickup-line mums, cold
reframe: The parent thinks the kids who are thriving have something special. Rotated 90°: the parents of those kids made one phone call.

name: 4A — The Mum Who Rang
headline: She made one phone call. It changed Tuesdays.
body: Jess rang Tutero on a Thursday. By Tuesday, her daughter had a real teacher — not a name from a portal, but a person Anna had matched to her. Now Tuesdays are the best day of the week. One phone call. That's all it took.
audience: Pickup-line mums, cold
reframe: The parent thinks making a change is hard. Rotated 90°: it's one phone call, one Tuesday, and you'll know.

name: 4B — Less Worry. More Anna.
headline: Less worry. More Anna.
body: Most tutoring services give you a portal. We give you Anna. She matches your child with the right teacher, reads every lesson report, and picks up when you call. That's what 1-on-1 is supposed to mean.
audience: Pickup-line mums, cold
reframe: The parent carries guilt about whether they're doing enough. Rotated 90°: one phone call dissolves the guilt because Anna carries it now.

name: 3A — Meet Your Teacher First
headline: Meet your teacher. Then book a lesson.
body:
Most tutoring services hand you a name. We hand you a teacher.

Meet them, talk to them, and decide. No contract. No upfront payment. Just one Tuesday and a real human teacher who already knows what your child needs.
audience: Trust-wounded parents, cold
reframe: Parents think they pick a tutor from a list. Rotated 90°: you audition a teacher. You interview them.

name: 3B — Hannah Got In
headline: 30,000 teachers apply. Hannah got in.
body:
Hannah teaches Year 7–10 maths from her home in Melbourne. She is one of the 1.3% of teachers we accept each year — out of more than 30,000 who apply.

If you'd like to meet her on a Tuesday at 5pm, we can introduce you this week.
audience: Trust-wounded parents, cold
reframe: The 1.3% number is usually a brag. Rotated 90°: it's a promise about who's about to teach your child.

name: 5A — You Interview Them
headline: Meet your teacher before you commit.
body: Most tutoring services hand you a name. We hand you a teacher. Meet them, talk to them, and decide. No contract. No upfront payment. Just one Tuesday and a real human teacher who already knows what your child needs.
audience: Trust-wounded parents, cold
reframe: Parents think they book a tutor sight unseen. Rotated 90°: you interview them first, like hiring someone for the most important job.

name: 5B — Tuesday at 5pm
headline: She's free this Tuesday at 5pm.
body: Priya teaches Year 9–12 English from her home in Brisbane. She is one of the 1.3% of teachers we accept each year. If you'd like to meet her this Tuesday at 5pm, we can introduce you this week.
audience: Trust-wounded parents, cold
reframe: Parents think starting tutoring is a big, vague commitment. Rotated 90°: it's a specific day, a specific time, and a specific person.

name: 6A — He Used To Love Maths
headline: He used to love maths.
body:
He used to come home and talk about what he learned. Then Year 7 hit, and the shrug replaced the stories. He's not failing — he's just… quiet.

What if the problem wasn't maths? What if it was that nobody in a room of thirty noticed he'd stopped trying? One teacher. One hour a week. That's all it takes to find out.
audience: Parents of disengaged Year 7–10 kids, cold
reframe: The child didn't lose ability. They lost someone who noticed. One teacher who pays attention changes everything.

name: 6B — The Shrug
headline: You ask how school was. He shrugs.
body:
You ask him how school was. He shrugs. You check the report card — 'satisfactory.' You know your child better than the report does. Something shifted, and you can feel it.

A Tutero teacher doesn't just teach the subject. They teach your child — knowing exactly where they're stuck, what they're avoiding, and what will bring them back. One lesson. That's all it takes to see if the light turns back on.
audience: Parents of disengaged Year 7–10 kids, cold
reframe: The parent thinks their child has lost interest in learning. Rotated 90°: the child lost someone who noticed — not the interest itself.

name: 7A — What The Report Card Doesn't Say
headline: The report card said 'satisfactory.'
body:
The report card said 'satisfactory.' But you know what satisfactory looks like at home — it looks like a kid who's stopped trying.

Tutero matches your child with a real teacher who notices when they check out, knows what they're stuck on, and sends you a written report after every lesson. You don't have to wonder anymore.
audience: Parents of disengaged Year 7–10 kids, cold
reframe: The school says the child is fine. Rotated 90°: 'fine' is the sound of a child the system stopped seeing.

name: 7B — She Noticed Before The School Did
headline: Her maths teacher said she was keeping up. Her Tutero teacher noticed she was guessing.
body:
Her maths teacher said she was keeping up. Her Tutero teacher noticed she was guessing. One sends a grade. The other sends a lesson report.

When someone actually pays attention to your child — not the class, not the cohort, your child — you find out things the school can't tell you.
audience: Parents of disengaged Year 7–10 kids, cold
reframe: The school gives grades. Rotated 90°: Tutero gives attention. The difference is what you find out.

name: 8A — Mum Can We Keep Doing Tuesdays
headline: She asked. Not you. Her.
body:
Every parent braces for the pushback. "I don't want a tutor." "It's boring." "Can I just do it later?"

Then one Tuesday, your phone buzzes. Not a complaint. A request. "Can we keep doing Tuesdays?" That's what happens when the teacher actually knows your child — not just the subject.
audience: Parents of screen-time / resistant kids, cold
reframe: Parents expect resistance. Rotated 90°: what if the child actually asked for more?

name: 8B — The Text You Didn't Expect
headline: Mum, can we keep doing Tuesdays?
body:
Every parent braces for the pushback. Then one Tuesday, your phone buzzes. Not a complaint. A request.

That's what happens when the teacher actually knows your child — not just the subject. One teacher. Every week. The same person who remembers what your child struggled with last Tuesday.
audience: Parents of screen-time / resistant kids, cold
reframe: The child's request IS the testimonial. No parent expects it. That's what makes it stop the scroll.

name: 10A — The Table Is Just For Dinner
headline: Since Tuesdays, the table is just for dinner.
body:
Tuesday night used to end in tears. Hers, sometimes his, occasionally both.

Now Tuesdays end with a lesson report that says "Sophie asked three questions unprompted today." And dinner is just dinner again.

One teacher. One hour a week. That's all it takes to give the dinner table back.
audience: Homework-battle parents, cold
reframe: Parents think tutoring improves grades. Rotated 90°: tutoring gives back the dinner table.

name: 10B — You're Not Supposed To Be The Teacher
headline: You're not supposed to be the teacher.
body:
You're not a maths teacher. You're not supposed to explain fractions at 7pm with one eye on the pasta. You're supposed to be the parent.

Tutero gives your child a real teacher — the same one, every week — so you can go back to being mum. Or dad. Or just the person who makes dinner without a textbook on the counter.
audience: Homework-battle parents, cold
reframe: Parents feel guilty they can't teach the subject. Rotated 90°: it was never your job.

name: 11A — You're Not The Only Parent Who Searched This
headline: You googled this at 11pm. We know.
body:
You googled it at 11pm because you didn't know who else to ask. The school says he's fine. The report card says "satisfactory." But you know.

4,000+ families started with that same late-night search — and found a tutor who actually pays attention. A real person named Anna will pick up when you call.
audience: Late-night searchers, anxious parents, cold
reframe: Parents think they're alone in their worry. Rotated 90°: 4,000+ families started with the same search.

name: 13A — She's Not A Better Mum
headline: The mum whose kid is thriving isn't doing more than you.
body:
She rang one number on a Thursday afternoon. A real person picked up — not a call centre, not a chatbot. Anna.

Anna matched her daughter with a tutor. Now every Tuesday there's a lesson, and every Tuesday there's a report. She stopped worrying because she stopped guessing.

One phone call. That's the whole difference.
audience: Pickup-line mums, cold
reframe: The parent thinks other mums are better parents. Rotated 90°: they're not better — they made one call.

name: 13B — Different Thursday
headline: Same school. Same class. Different Thursday.
body:
Both kids sit in the same classroom. Both mums do the same pickup. But on Thursday evening, one mum gets a lesson report that says exactly what her child understood and exactly what she's stuck on.

The other mum asks "how was school?" and gets a shrug.

The difference wasn't the child. It was one phone call.
audience: Pickup-line mums, cold
reframe: Parents think the difference is the child. Rotated 90°: the difference is what happens after school — one mum gets a report, the other gets a shrug.

name: 14A — She Already Knows
headline: She's not guessing. She gets told. Every Tuesday.
body:
The mum at the gate who never seems worried? She's not calmer than you. She's not luckier. She just has someone who tells her, every single week, exactly how her child is doing — not a grade on a piece of paper, but a real tutor's real words.

Her education manager's name is Anna. Anna picks up when she rings. That's the difference.
audience: Pickup-line mums, cold
reframe: Parents think the calm mum is naturally unbothered. Rotated 90°: she's informed, not calm. Information replaced the worry.

name: 14B — Every Mum Has The Same Worry
headline: Every mum at the gate has the same worry. One of them did something about it.
body:
You all worry. You all wonder. Is she keeping up? Is he falling behind? Is the school actually telling me the truth?

The difference isn't who worries more. It's who picked up the phone. When she rang Tutero, a real person answered. Matched her child with a real tutor. And now every Tuesday she gets a report — not a grade, a real account of what happened.

She still worries. Just less.
audience: Pickup-line mums, cold
reframe: The worry is universal. Rotated 90°: what separates the calm mums from the worried ones isn't a personality type — it's a single phone call.

name: 15A — One Tuesday. That's It.
headline: No contract. No commitment. Just one Tuesday.
body:
You don't have to commit to tutoring. You just have to try one Tuesday.

One lesson. One hour. One real tutor who already knows where your child is stuck.

If it doesn't feel right, you walk away. No contract. No follow-up calls. No strings.

That's what "open door" actually means.
audience: Price-sensitive parents, first-time tutoring buyers, cold
reframe: Parents think tutoring means a big financial commitment. Rotated 90°: it's one lesson on one Tuesday. The commitment is one hour, not a contract.

name: 15B — The First Lesson Is Proof
headline: The first lesson is proof. Not a sales pitch.
body:
The first lesson is proof. Not a sales pitch. No lock-in, no strings attached.

A real tutor, teaching your child for one hour, and a written lesson report that tells you exactly what happened — what they understood, what they struggled with, what's next.

If you're not convinced, you stop. If you are, Tuesday becomes the best day of the week.
audience: Skeptical parents who've tried other tutoring, cold
reframe: Parents want guarantees before they spend. Rotated 90°: the proof comes AFTER the first lesson — a written report. You only pay once to find out.

name: 16A — Every Family Deserves This
headline: 4,000+ Australian families already have this.
body:
You might think 1-on-1 tutoring with a real education manager is for a certain kind of family. It isn't.

4,000+ Australian families — across every suburb, every school type, every budget — have a Tutero tutor. Same tutor every week. A real person who picks up when you call. And not one of them signed a contract.

Good tutoring shouldn't be a luxury. At Tutero, it isn't.
audience: Budget-conscious families, cold
reframe: Parents think 1:1 tutoring with an education manager is premium-only. Rotated 90°: 4,000+ families already have it — and not one signed a contract.

name: 16B — You're Not Locked In
headline: No sign-up fee. No minimum term. No tricks.
body:
No sign-up fee. No minimum term. No "we'll charge you if you don't cancel within 14 days."

Tutero doesn't lock families in because we don't have to. A real tutor, matched to your child, teaching them one-on-one every week — that's what earns a second Tuesday. Not a contract.

If it isn't working, you stop. If it is, you'll know.
audience: Parents burned by lock-in contracts, cold
reframe: The thing that makes tutoring feel expensive isn't the price — it's the lock-in. Remove the contract and the price shrinks in the parent's mind.

name: 17A — Worth It.
headline: Three weeks in, she stopped saying 'I don't get it.'
body:
Three weeks in, she stopped saying "I don't get it." Six weeks in, she started asking about next Tuesday.

You don't notice the number on the invoice anymore. You notice the kid who came back.

One tutor. One hour. Every week. The only thing you'll wonder is why you waited.
audience: Parents on the fence, cold
reframe: Parents calculate 'worth it' in grades. Rotated 90°: the return is the child who stops avoiding homework and starts asking about next week.

name: 17B — I Wish I Hadn't Waited
headline: I spent six months telling myself we couldn't afford it.
body:
Every parent who books a Tutero lesson says the same thing: "I wish we'd started sooner."

Not because it's cheap — because it's worth it. One real tutor. One hour a week. A written report after every session that tells you exactly where your child is.

No contract. No tricks. Just proof, every Tuesday, that something is working.
audience: Procrastinating parents, cold
reframe: Parents procrastinate because the cost feels abstract and the benefit uncertain. Rotated 90°: every week you wait is a week your child doesn't have someone paying attention.

name: 18A — Chosen, Not Hired
headline: Your child's tutor was chosen from 30,000 applicants.
body:
30,000 tutors applied to teach with Tutero last year. We accepted 1.3%.

Your child doesn't get a name from a list. They get a tutor who was chosen — someone who passed a teaching exam, a background check, and a training program before they sat down with your child.

One tutor. One hour. Every week. The same person who remembers what your child struggled with last Tuesday.
audience: Trust-wounded parents, cold
reframe: Parents think 'qualified' means a credential on paper. Rotated 90°: qualified means being chosen from 30,000 applicants. It's not a credential — it's a selection.

name: 18B — 30,000 Applied. She Got In.
headline: 30,000 tutors applied. Sophie got in.
body:
30,000 tutors applied to Tutero last year. Sophie got in.

Sophie teaches Year 7–10 maths from her home in Melbourne. She is one of the 1.3% of tutors we accept each year — out of more than 30,000 who apply.

Every tutor on our faculty passed a teaching exam, a background check, and completed our training program. Your child doesn't get a random name. They get Sophie.
audience: Trust-wounded parents, cold
reframe: The 1.3% number is usually a brag. Rotated 90°: it's a promise about who's about to teach your child. Making it personal — 'Sophie got in' — transforms a stat into a person.

name: 18C — Meet the 1.3%
headline: These are the tutors other families chose for their kids.
body:
These are the tutors other families chose for their kids.

Tutero accepts 1.3% of tutors who apply — from more than 30,000 each year. Every one passed a teaching exam, a working-with-children check, and completed our training program.

You don't pick a name from a list. You meet your tutor, talk to them, and decide. No contract. No upfront payment.
audience: Trust-wounded parents, cold
reframe: Parents think they pick a tutor from a faceless list. Rotated 90°: you meet them face to face. Six named humans with subjects and year levels — that's what 1.3% looks like.

name: 18D — I Finally Know
headline: I finally know who's teaching my daughter.
body:
She'd tried two tutoring services before Tutero. Both times, she had no idea who was actually teaching her daughter.

When she rang Tutero, a real person picked up. Matched her daughter with Sarah — one of the 1.3% of tutors we accept. Now every Tuesday, she gets a written lesson report that tells her exactly what happened.

She doesn't wonder anymore.
audience: Parents burned by previous tutoring, cold
reframe: Parents have been let down by faceless tutoring companies. Rotated 90°: 'finally' acknowledges every time before when she didn't know. The testimonial sells peace of mind, not grades.

name: 18E — 4,000+ Families Chose Their Tutor
headline: 4,000+ Australian families already chose their tutor.
body:
4,000+ Australian families already have a Tutero tutor.

Every tutor on our faculty was chosen from more than 30,000 applicants. 1.3% acceptance rate. Every one passed a teaching exam, a background check, and completed training before sitting down with a child.

No contract. No sign-up fee. Just one lesson to find out if this is the right tutor for your family.
audience: Social-proof-seeking parents, cold
reframe: Parents need social proof before committing. Rotated 90°: three real families at their kitchen tables is volume of evidence — one photo is anecdote, three is pattern.

name: 18F — The Numbers Speak
headline: Not every tutor makes the cut. Here's what the numbers say.
body:
30,000+ tutors apply to Tutero every year. We accept 1.3%.

4,000+ Australian families already trust us. 200,000+ lessons delivered. Every tutor passed a teaching exam, a background check, and completed our training program.

No contract. No lock-in. A real person picks up when you call.
audience: Analytical parents, cold
reframe: Parents want evidence, not emotion. Rotated 90°: the numbers themselves are the visual. Data as design — when the proof stack is more compelling than any photo.

name: 19C — One Lesson
headline: One lesson. That's all we're asking.
body:
One hour with a real tutor who already knows where your child is stuck.

If it doesn't feel right, you walk away. No invoice. No follow-up. No strings.

We're not asking you to commit to tutoring. We're asking you to try one Tuesday.
audience: Hesitant parents, first-time tutoring buyers, cold
reframe: Parents see tutoring as a big commitment decision. Rotated 90°: it's one lesson. The emptiness of the ad mirrors how little we're asking — the visual weight matches the ask.
layout_blueprint: Style Inspiration #12 — The Negative Space Hero

name: 20A — She Has Every App
headline: She downloaded seven apps. He still doesn't get fractions.
body:
You've tried the apps. You've bookmarked the YouTube channels. You've sat with him while he clicked through exercises that beeped and buzzed but changed nothing.

Here's what an app can't do: notice when your child's eyes go blank.

Tutero matches your child with a real tutor — the same person, every week — who knows exactly where they're stuck and adjusts in real time. Not an algorithm. A human being who picks up where the screen left off.

Book a 1-on-1 lesson →
audience: Tech-savvy parents who've tried educational apps, cold
reframe: Parent thinks their child needs more content or a better app. Rotated 90°: the child doesn't need more content — they need one person who notices when they stop understanding.

name: 20B — Apps Don't Notice
headline: An app can teach a formula. It can't notice your child gave up.
body:
Khan Academy is free. Mathspace is clever. YouTube has a video for everything.

But none of them can notice when your child stops trying. None of them adjust. None of them know your child's name.

Tutero matches your child with one tutor — the same person, every week — who notices the moment they go quiet. Because attention isn't content. It's a person.

Book a 1-on-1 lesson →
audience: Parents who've exhausted free digital learning resources, cold
reframe: Parent believes information access = education. Rotated 90°: information isn't the problem — nobody is watching their child learn. The stacked headline makes the antithesis visual.
layout_blueprint: Style Inspiration #4 — The Stacked Headline

name: 20C — The Screen Can't See Him
headline: He does the exercises. He watches the videos. His marks are still dropping.
body:
He does the exercises. He watches the videos. He even gets the practice questions right sometimes.

But his marks are still dropping. Because content isn't the problem. Nobody is watching him learn.

Tutero gives your child one tutor — not a playlist, not a progress bar — a person who knows where he's stuck and won't move on until he's got it.

Book a 1-on-1 lesson →
audience: Parents whose children use apps but marks still drop, cold
reframe: Parent sees child doing the work and assumes it should be enough. Rotated 90°: doing exercises isn't learning — learning requires someone watching.

name: 21A — Sunday Nights
headline: It starts around 4pm on Sunday.
body:
You know the feeling. It starts around 4pm on Sunday. Your child gets quieter. The stomach ache appears. The 'I don't feel well' that vanishes by Tuesday.

It's not that school is too hard. It's that nobody there makes them feel like they can do it.

Tutero matches your child with one tutor — the same person, every week — who already knows where they're stuck before the lesson starts. One hour where someone sees them. That's what changes Monday morning.

Book a 1-on-1 lesson →
audience: Parents of anxious primary/early secondary children, cold
reframe: Parent thinks the child dreads school because it's too hard. Rotated 90°: the dread isn't about difficulty — it's about going somewhere nobody sees you.

name: 21B — Monday Feels Different
headline: She used to love school. Now Sunday evenings are heavy.
body:
She used to love school. Now Sunday evenings are heavy. Backpack sits by the door. She checks the timetable like she's bracing.

Nothing happened — not really. She just stopped feeling like anyone there noticed her.

Tutero matches your daughter with one tutor who knows her name, knows where she's stuck, and shows up the same time every week. It's one hour. But it changes what Monday morning feels like.

Book a 1-on-1 lesson →
audience: Parents of daughters who've lost confidence at school, cold
reframe: Parent thinks confidence loss is about difficulty. Rotated 90°: confidence left when the child became invisible. The horizontal split shows the contrast — dark mood vs. light solution.
layout_blueprint: Style Inspiration #6 — The Horizontal Split

name: 21C — Nobody Noticed
headline: Nobody noticed. Until someone did.
body:
In a class of thirty, some children learn to never raise their hand. They sit quietly. They get 'satisfactory.' They stop asking questions because nobody answers them anyway.

Tutero is one person who notices. Same tutor, same time, every week. They know your child's name. They know where they're stuck. And they don't move on until it clicks.

Book a 1-on-1 lesson →
audience: Parents of quiet, compliant children who've become invisible at school, cold
reframe: Parent assumes school is fine because child doesn't complain. Rotated 90°: silence isn't contentment — it's invisibility. The oversized statement makes the void unmissable.
layout_blueprint: Style Inspiration #1 — The Oversized Statement

name: 22A — I Wish I Hadn't Waited
headline: I wish I hadn't waited until Year 9.
body:
I saw the gap in Year 5. She went from 'I love maths' to 'I'm just not a maths person' in two years.

By Year 9, I couldn't help her anymore. I'd sit next to her and we'd both stare at the same question.

Three lessons in, her tutor found the exact concept she missed in Year 6. Three years of 'I can't do maths' — and the answer was one concept she never learned.

Book a 1-on-1 lesson →
audience: Parents who've been meaning to get tutoring for years, warm/cold
reframe: Parent thinks tutoring is a permanent commitment. Rotated 90°: the gap has a start point — one tutor can find it and close it.

name: 22B — The Gap Is Open
headline: Right now, there's a gap opening.
body:
Right now, there's a gap opening. It's small. Maybe it's just a few marks. Maybe it's just 'I don't like maths anymore.'

But gaps don't close themselves. They get normal. And normal becomes 'that's just how she is.'

One tutor. One lesson a week. Same person who knows exactly where the gap started. That's all it takes before 'small gap' becomes 'she's behind.'

Book a 1-on-1 lesson →
audience: Parents noticing early signs of academic decline, cold
reframe: Parent thinks the dip is temporary and will self-correct. Rotated 90°: gaps don't close — they normalise. The urgency is now, not later.

name: 22C — Three Years Later
headline: Year 5, she loved school. Year 7, she stopped talking about it. Year 9, she said 'I'm just not a maths person.'
body:
Year 5, she loved school. Year 7, she stopped talking about it. Year 9, she said 'I'm just not a maths person.'

The gap opened three years ago. It didn't close because nobody was watching it.

Tutero matches your child with one tutor who finds exactly where it started — and works backwards until it clicks. No contract. No lock-in. Start with one lesson.

Book a 1-on-1 lesson →
audience: Parents who waited too long and now feel regret, warm
reframe: Parent thinks the child has changed. Rotated 90°: the child didn't change — nobody was watching the gap. The storyboard makes the timeline visible and urgent.
layout_blueprint: Style Inspiration #11 — The Storyboard Strip

name: 23A — She Had a Tutor Like That Once
headline: Remember when she loved learning? That tutor left.
body:
She had a tutor in Year 3 who changed everything. Mrs Chen, or Mr Davis — you remember their name because your child came home different.

Then they moved. Or your child moved up. And that feeling went with them.

Tutero matches your child with one tutor — from the top 1.3% — who stays. Same person, same time, every week. Not a rotation. Not a substitute. The one who already knows her.

Book a 1-on-1 lesson →
audience: Parents whose child clicked with a tutor then lost them, cold
reframe: Parent thinks good tutors are rare and temporary. Rotated 90°: the problem isn't finding someone good — it's keeping them. Same tutor, same time, every week.

name: 23B — The One Who Stays
headline: Every child has had one person who made them feel smart. Then they left.
body:
Every child has had one person who made them feel smart. A tutor. A coach. An uncle. Someone who saw them.

Then they left. Not because they didn't care — because that's how life works.

Tutero is the one who stays. Same tutor, same time, every week. Chosen from the top 1.3% of 30,000+ applicants. WWCC verified. And they're not going anywhere.

Book a 1-on-1 lesson →
audience: Parents who value consistency and connection for their child, cold
reframe: Parent fears another revolving door. Rotated 90°: Tutero is the opposite of temporary. The inset frame around the tutor makes them feel permanent, framed, chosen.
layout_blueprint: Style Inspiration #5 — The Inset Frame

name: 23C — Every Week. Same Person.
headline: Finally, someone who actually knows my daughter.
body:
The last tutoring service gave us a different person every second week. My daughter had to re-explain where she was stuck every time. It wasn't tutoring. It was a revolving door.

Tutero matched her with one tutor — Sophie — who's been with her for six months. Same time every Tuesday. She knows what my daughter struggles with before the lesson starts.

That's not a feature. That's the whole point.

Book a 1-on-1 lesson →
audience: Switchers from other tutoring services, warm
reframe: Parent expects tutor churn as normal. Rotated 90°: consistency isn't a feature — it's the whole product. The testimonial makes another parent say it.

name: 24A — Same Parents. Different Child.
headline: What worked for your first won't work for your second.
body:
Your first child did the homework without being asked. Your second needs you sitting next to them. Same house. Same school. Same parents.

Different child.

Tutero starts by understanding your child — not their sibling, not their class, not their year level. One tutor who learns how they think. One education manager who guides you. Because the strategy that worked for one was never going to work for the other.

Book a 1-on-1 lesson →
audience: Multi-child parents whose second child learns differently, cold
reframe: Parent assumes what worked for one child should work for the other. Rotated 90°: different children need different attention — not borrowed strategies.

name: 24B — Built For Her
headline: Your older one gets it on the first explanation. Your younger one needs three.
body:
Your older one gets it on the first explanation. Your younger one needs three, and they need them different each time.

That's not a deficit. That's a different mind.

Tutero doesn't give your child the same lesson plan as everyone else. They build one — from scratch — for the way your child actually thinks. One tutor. One education manager. One plan that's hers.

Book a 1-on-1 lesson →
audience: Parents comparing siblings, feeling guilty about the difference, cold
reframe: Parent sees the second child as slower. Rotated 90°: they're not slower — they need a plan built for their mind, not their sibling's. The negative space mirrors the individuality.
layout_blueprint: Style Inspiration #12 — The Negative Space Hero

name: 24C — They're Not The Same Kid
headline: You love them the same. But they don't learn the same.
body:
You love them the same. But they don't learn the same.

One reads ahead. The other reads the same page three times. One asks for help. The other pretends they don't need it.

Tutero doesn't give your second child a diluted version of what worked for the first. We start fresh. One tutor who learns how they think. One education manager who knows what you've tried. A plan that's theirs — not their sibling's.

Book a 1-on-1 lesson →
audience: Multi-child parents feeling guilt about unequal outcomes, cold
reframe: Parent feels guilty that one child thrives and the other doesn't. Rotated 90°: equal love doesn't mean equal learning — each child needs their own plan. The polaroid collage shows different moments, different children.

name: 25A — Most Qualified Tutors
headline: 1:1 tutoring with one of Australia's most qualified tutors.
body:

Weekly 1-on-1 lessons. A lesson report after every session. No contract.

audience: Parents worried about tutor quality, cold

headline: Your tutor already knows where your child is stuck before the lesson starts.

Every Tuesday, your child's tutor walks in already knowing what they got wrong last week, where the gaps are, and what to teach next.

That's not a guess. That's what 1-on-1 is supposed to mean.

Book a 1-on-1 lesson →

headline: No contract. No upfront payment. Just proof after every lesson.
body:

You know the shrug. The 'it's fine' that means it isn't. The door closing earlier every evening.

This is what 1-on-1 tutoring is supposed to feel like. Not a portal. Not an inbox. A person who knows your child's name.

name: 28A — Expensive Homework Supervision
headline: Most tutoring is just expensive homework supervision.
body: If your tutor doesn't know where your kid got stuck, they're just doing more of what already isn't working. Tutero starts at the exact spot she's stuck. Not chapter one.
audience: Switcher parents who've tried tutoring before, cold
reframe: Parent thinks their child needs more tutoring. Rotated 90°: they need the right starting point, not more hours.
layout_blueprint: Workhorse with strong eyebrow (eyebrow carries the reframe; hero is the answer)

name: 28B — Not Chapter One
headline: We don't start at chapter one. We start where she got stuck.
body: Your kid didn't fall behind on day one. She fell behind at a specific moment — the week fractions got weird. That's where Tutero starts.
audience: Switcher parents who've tried tutoring before, cold
reframe: Parent thinks catching up starts at the beginning of the textbook. Rotated 90°: it starts at the specific moment things stopped making sense.
layout_blueprint: Workhorse (eyebrow + gradient hero + scene photo + ticks)

name: 28C — The Reason The Last Tutor Didn't Work
headline: The reason the last tutor didn't work.
body: They picked up wherever the class was. More of the same. Tutero finds the exact spot she's stuck — then fills the gap. One tutor. One plan.
audience: Switcher parents who've tried tutoring before, cold
reframe: Parent blames the child or the tutor's quality. Rotated 90°: the tutor was fine — the starting point was wrong.

name: 28D — A Better Starting Point
headline: More tutoring isn't the answer. A better starting point is.
body: Parents ring Tutero after a tutor who didn't work, a centre that didn't stick, YouTube at 10pm. The answer was never more help. It was help that starts in the right place.
audience: Switcher parents who've tried tutoring before, cold
reframe: Parent thinks the solution is volume (more hours, more apps, more help). Rotated 90°: volume is the problem — starting point is the fix.

name: 35L — The Rejection Grid (Experimental)
headline: We said no to 29,610 tutors this year. She wasn't one of them.
body:
We accept 1.3% of tutors from 30,000 applications a year. Your child gets one of the ones who made it.

Weekly 1-on-1 lessons. A lesson report after every session. No contract.

Book a 1-on-1 lesson →
audience: Parents worried about tutor quality, cold
reframe: Visual metaphor for selectivity. 70 grey dots = rejected applicants. 1 blue dot with a face = your child's tutor. The density of rejection makes the single acceptance feel precious. No template or style inspiration does this.
layout_blueprint: Novel — The Rejection Grid (dot matrix as visual metaphor for filtering)

name: 35M — The Versus Bar (Experimental)
headline: More selective than you think. Tutero 1.3% vs Harvard 3.2% vs Industry ~80%.
body:
Your child's tutor passed a bar that 98.7% didn't.

No contract. No upfront payment.

Book a 1-on-1 lesson →
audience: Parents worried about tutor quality, cold
reframe: Cross-category comparator made visual (Thinking Move 2). Anchoring Tutero's 1.3% against Harvard's 3.2% shifts the parent's mental model from 'tutoring company' to 'institution'. The industry's ~80% makes the contrast visceral. No photo — the data IS the scroll-stop.
layout_blueprint: Novel — The Versus Bar (comparative bar chart as primary creative)

name: 35N — The Tutor Dossier (Experimental)
headline: This is your child's tutor. Emma W. — B.Sc (Hons), Top 1.3%, ★ 4.9.
body:
We accept 1.3% of tutors from 30,000 applications a year. Every tutor is WWCC verified, university qualified, and rated by parents.

Weekly 1-on-1 lessons. A lesson report after every session. No contract.

Book a 1-on-1 lesson →
audience: Parents worried about tutor quality, cold
reframe: The ad looks like an internal vetting file the parent was never meant to see. Structured fields (qualification, acceptance %, rating, WWCC) create institutional trust. The parent feels like they're looking behind the curtain, not being sold to.
layout_blueprint: Novel — The Tutor Dossier (structured profile card as primary creative)

name: 35O — The Funnel Cascade (Experimental)
headline: How we find your child's tutor. 30,000 → 390 → 1.
body:
30,000 applications a year. 390 tutors accepted. 1 matched to your child.

Weekly 1-on-1 lessons. A lesson report after every session. No contract.

Book a 1-on-1 lesson →
audience: Parents worried about tutor quality, cold
reframe: Visual narrative of progressive filtering. Bars get narrower and more colourful as the funnel tightens. The parent reads it as a story — mass → selection → their child. The tutor photo at the endpoint makes the '1' feel personal, not abstract.
layout_blueprint: Novel — The Funnel Cascade (progressive narrowing bars with portrait payoff)

name: 36A — Confidence Testimonial
headline: Grow your child's confidence this term.
body:
"We have been extremely impressed with Tutero. Excellent service and my daughter really enjoys her sessions and is getting a lot out of them. 10/10 from us."

1-on-1 tutoring with one of Australia's top 1.3% of tutors — matched to your child, every week.

Book a 1-on-1 lesson →
audience: Parents seeking confidence for their child, cold
reframe: Parent worries tutoring is just more academic pressure. Rotated 90°: tutoring builds confidence, not just grades. The testimonial is the proof — a real parent saying 'my daughter enjoys it.'
layout_blueprint: Full-bleed photo + headline + testimonial card (Superpower-inspired)
